profiles.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Aaron enters the biblical story as Moses’ older brother from the tribe of Levi , appointed by God to be Moses’ spokesman in the confrontation with Pharaoh (Ex 4:14–16; 7:1–2).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Abel is the second named human child in Scripture, born to Adam and Eve (Genesis 4:2).
Name note: Her name is usually spelled Abigail in English Bibles. Primary narrative: 1 Samuel 25 .
1) Story Summary (chronological) Abraham (first called Abram ) enters the biblical story as a man living among the peoples of Mesopotamia, descended from Shem through Terah (Gen 11:10–27).
1) Story Summary (chronological) Absalom is a son of King David whose life traces a tragic arc: favored prince → avenger → estranged son → charismatic usurper → slain rebel.
1) Story Summary Adam is the first man, directly created by God, placed in Eden to “work” and “keep” the garden, given a clear command regarding the tree of the knowledge of good a...
1) Story Summary (chronological) Agabus is a prophet in the early church whose ministry is recorded in Acts in two decisive scenes.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Agrippa I appears in Scripture as “Herod the king” who persecutes the Jerusalem church during the early years of Acts.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Herod Agrippa II enters the biblical narrative late in Acts, during Paul’s custody at Caesarea after Paul’s arrest in Jerusalem.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Amos appears as a prophet unexpectedly raised up from ordinary work, sent with a severe message to the Northern Kingdom (Israel) during a season of...
(Simon Peter’s brother) 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Andrew enters the biblical story as a disciple of John the Baptist.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Anna appears briefly but powerfully at the climax of Luke’s infancy narrative.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Annas enters the biblical record as a leading priestly figure during the opening of John the Baptist’s ministry: Luke dates John’s prophetic call “...
1) Story Summary (chronological) Apollos enters the biblical narrative during Paul’s wider missionary activity in the mid–first century.
1) Story Summary Aquila enters the biblical narrative as a Jewish believer (or soon-to-be believer) displaced by imperial policy.

1) Story Summary (Chronological) Aristarchus enters the biblical narrative during Paul’s ministry in Ephesus, when a city-wide uproar erupts because the gospel threatens the local idol-making economy.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Barnabas first enters the biblical narrative in the early Jerusalem church as a trusted disciple known for remarkable generosity and spiritual maturity.
Name note (important): “Bartholomew” appears in the NT only in lists of the Twelve (Matthew 10:3; Mark 3:18; Luke 6:14; Acts 1:13).
1) Story Summary (chronological) Boaz enters the biblical narrative during the “days when the judges ruled” (Ruth 1:1), in Bethlehem of Judah (Ruth 1:1–2; 2:4).

1) Story Summary (Chronological) Caiaphas appears in the Gospels as the high priest who plays a central role in the Jewish leadership’s opposition to Jesus, especially in the events that lead to the crucifixion.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Cain is the firstborn son of Adam and Eve, born after humanity’s expulsion from Eden (Gen 4:1).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Cornelius enters Scripture as a Roman centurion stationed in Caesarea —a Gentile military officer who nevertheless fears the God of Israel , prays...
1) Story Summary (chronological) Crispus enters the biblical narrative in Corinth during Paul’s ministry there on the second missionary journey (Acts 18:1–11).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Daniel’s story begins with his forced relocation from Judah to Babylon during the first wave of exile.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) David’s story begins as a young shepherd from Bethlehem, the youngest son of Jesse, chosen by God and anointed by Samuel to replace Saul as king (1 Sam 16:1–13).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Deborah appears during Israel’s cyclical period of the judges—after Joshua and before the monarchy—when “the people of Israel again did what was ev...
1) Story Summary (chronological) Demas appears briefly in Paul’s letters as one of Paul’s companions and ministry “fellow workers” during a season of imprisonment and active mission work.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Dionysius the Areopagite appears only once in Scripture, at the close of Paul’s ministry encounter in Athens.
Eleazar — Which Eleazar? “Eleazar” is a common name in Scripture. This study focuses on Eleazar son of Aaron , who became high priest after Aaron (Num 20:22–29).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Eli enters the biblical story as the priest presiding at the sanctuary in Shiloh during the waning days of the judges (1 Sam 1:3, 9).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Elijah enters Scripture abruptly as a prophetic messenger from the Lord during the reign of Ahab king of Israel , confronting national idolatry and...
1) Story Summary (chronological) Elisha enters the biblical narrative near the end of Elijah’s ministry.
Name note: “Elizabeth” in the New Testament refers to the wife of Zechariah and mother of John the Baptist (Luke 1).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Enoch (the descendant in Adam’s line through Seth) appears in the genealogy of Genesis 5.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Epaphras appears in the NT as a gospel minister closely connected to the churches in the Lycus Valley (Colossae, Laodicea, Hierapolis).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Epaphroditus appears in Paul’s letter to the Philippians as a trusted representative of the Philippian church and a devoted coworker of Paul.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Erastus appears briefly but meaningfully in the orbit of the apostle Paul as a trusted coworker who helps advance the mission through practical service and travel.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Esther’s story unfolds among the Jewish exiles who remained in the Persian Empire after the return to Judah had begun (cf.
1) Story Summary Eve is the first woman, created by God as a suitable helper for Adam, placed with him in Eden under God’s good rule and clear command (Genesis 2:18–25; 2:15–17).
1) Story Summary (chronological) Ezekiel is a priest turned prophet called by God in exile among the deported community in Babylon.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Ezra enters the biblical narrative during the Persian period, after Judah has returned from Babylonian exile and the temple has been rebuilt (Ezra 1–6).
Primary identity: Felix is the Roman governor (often translated “procurator” or “governor”) who presides over key hearings in Paul’s imprisonment at Caesarea .
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Festus enters the biblical narrative as the Roman governor who succeeds Felix in Judea, inheriting Paul’s unresolved imprisonment.
1) Story Summary (chronological, as best Scripture allows) The New Testament mentions “Gaius” in several contexts—likely referring to more than one man (the name was common).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Gallio appears briefly during Paul’s ministry in Corinth .
1) Story Summary (chronological, as Scripture reveals) Gamaliel appears briefly but memorably in the early church’s Jerusalem conflicts.

1) Story Summary (chronological) Gideon enters the biblical narrative during a dark cycle in Judges: Israel again “did what was evil,” and the LORD gave them into Midian’s hand for seven years (Judg 6:1).
1) Story Summary (chronological) Habakkuk is a prophet whose “story” is largely contained in the short book that bears his name.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Haggai appears suddenly in the post-exilic period as a covenant prosecutor and pastoral encourager.

1) Story Summary (Chronological) Hannah’s story unfolds in the opening chapters of 1 Samuel during the closing era of the judges, when Israel’s worship center was at Shiloh (Joshua 18:1; 1 Samuel 1:3).
1) Story Summary (chronological) Herod Antipas enters the Gospel narratives as the “Herod” who rules Galilee and Perea during Jesus’ public ministry.
Name note: In the New Testament, he is usually called simply “Herod” (Matt 2:1; Luke 1:5).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Herodias appears in the Gospel narratives connected to John the Baptist’s confrontation of Herod Antipas and John’s martyrdom.

1) Story Summary (Chronological) Hosea is a prophet called by God to minister primarily to the northern kingdom of Israel (often called Ephraim ) during a period of outward prosperity but deep covenant unfaithfulness.

1) Story Summary (chronological) Isaac is the long-promised “son of laughter,” born to Abraham and Sarah by God’s supernatural provision, and the covenant line is expressly carried through him (Gen 17:19; 21:1–3, 12).
1) Story Summary (chronological) Isaiah is the major prophet most closely associated with Judah and Jerusalem in the generations leading up to the Babylonian exile.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Jacob’s story begins in the patriarchal narratives as the younger twin son of Isaac and Rebekah (Gen 25:19–26).
James (Son of Alphaeus) — Bible Character Study A note on sources and uncertainty Scripture gives very little narrative detail about James the son of Alphaeus.
Important identification note: The New Testament mentions multiple men named James .
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Jeremiah is a prophet called by the LORD in Judah during the final decades before Jerusalem’s fall to Babylon.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Jesus Christ is introduced as the eternal Word/Son who was “with God” and “was God,” through whom all things were made (John 1:1–3; Col 1:15–17).
# Job — Bible Character Study ## 1) Story Summary (Chronological) **Where Job’s story begins and ends:** Job’s narrative begins with Job’s description and the heavenly council scen...
Note on identity: Several men named Joel appear in Scripture (e.g., Samuel’s son, civic leaders, genealogies).
Important identifiers: This study concerns John the apostle , brother of James (son of Zebedee) (Matt 4:21–22; Mark 1:19–20).

1) Story Summary (chronological) Where his story begins: John Mark first appears during the Jerusalem church’s early growth and persecution—at a moment when believers are gathered for prayer (Acts 12:12).
1) Story Summary (chronological) John the Baptist enters Scripture as the promised forerunner who prepares Israel for the Lord’s coming (Luke 1:13–17; Isa 40:3; Mal 3:1).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Jonah (“son of Amittai”) appears as a prophet of Israel during the reign of Jeroboam II (king of the northern kingdom), associated with a prophecy...
Scope note: This study is about Joseph the patriarch , son of Jacob/Israel (Genesis 37–50), not Joseph the husband of Mary (Matthew 1–2; Luke 1–2).
Note on identity: This is Joseph of Nazareth , husband of Mary, the legal father/guardian of Jesus (Matt 1–2; Luke 1–2)—not Joseph son of Jacob (Genesis).
1) Story Summary (chronological) Joseph of Arimathea enters the biblical narrative late—at the crucifixion and burial of Jesus.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Joshua (Heb. Yehoshua , “Yahweh saves”) enters Scripture as Moses’ aide during Israel’s wilderness years, then becomes Moses’ commissioned successo...
1) Story Summary (chronological) Josiah becomes king of Judah at eight years old after the assassination of his father Amon (2 Kgs 21:23–24; 22:1).

1) Story Summary Judas Iscariot appears in the Gospels as one of “the twelve” chosen by Jesus, entrusted with the group’s funds, and ultimately the betrayer who delivers Jesus into the hands of His enemies.
Jude — Bible Character Study (with “Jude/Judas” name clarification) Important name note: “Jude” is the English form often used to distinguish Judas (Greek Ioudas ) from Judas Iscariot .
Important note on names: The New Testament also includes a “Lazarus” in Jesus’ parable of the rich man and Lazarus (Luke 16:19–31).
Note on name ambiguity: “Levi” can refer to (1) Levi the patriarch , Jacob’s third son by Leah (Genesis), and (2) Levi the tax collector , also called Matthew (Gospels).
Note on sources: Scripture gives only a handful of direct biographical references to Luke (Col 4:14; Phm 24; 2 Tim 4:11).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Lydia appears in the book of Acts during Paul’s second missionary journey, when the Spirit directs the mission team into Macedonia (Acts 16:6–10).
Malachi — Bible Character Study Note on genre: Malachi is presented almost entirely through his prophetic messages rather than biographical narrative.
Identity note: The NT “Mark” is almost certainly the same person as “John, whose other name was Mark” (often called John Mark ) (Acts 12:12, 12:25).
1) Story Summary (chronological) Where her story begins and ends in Scripture Begins: When Jesus enters a village and Martha welcomes Him into her house (Luke 10:38).
(This study focuses on Mary of Nazareth , the mother of Jesus—distinguished from other Marys in the NT, e.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Where her story begins: in the Galilean phase of Jesus’ ministry, when she is identified as a woman from whom Jesus cast out seven demons and who b...

profiles.profile_name: Mary of Clopas (Mary the wife of Clopas)
profiles.source_path: NTCharacters/Mary of Clopas (Mary the wife of Clopas).md
profiles.summary: Name note: “Mary of Clopas” appears explicitly in John 19:25 . Many scholars connect her with “Mary the mother of James and Joses/Joseph” in the Synoptics ( Matthew 27:56; Mark 15:...
Note on name/spelling: You wrote “Mathew.” I’m treating this as Matthew (Greek: Matthaios ), the disciple also called Levi in the call narrative (Mark 2:14; Luke 5:27).
1) Story Summary (chronological) Matthias appears in the Bible at a critical “in-between” moment: after Jesus’ ascension and before Pentecost.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Methuselah belongs to the antediluvian (“before the flood”) patriarchs listed in Genesis’ genealogy from Adam to Noah.
Micah (the prophet, “the Morashtite”) Disambiguation: “Micah” can refer to multiple people in Scripture (e.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Miriam is the sister of Moses and Aaron and a key female leader in Israel’s exodus generation.

Moses — Robust Bible Character Study (Scripture-Primary) 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Moses’ story begins under Israel’s oppression in Egypt, when a pharaoh decrees death for Hebrew male infants (Ex 1:8–22).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Nahum is introduced as “Nahum of Elkosh” and identified with “the book of the vision of Nahum” (Nahum 1:1).
Disambiguation: This study is about Nathan the prophet in King David’s court (2 Samuel; 1 Kings; 1 Chronicles), not Nathan the son of David (e.
Primary texts: Nehemiah (esp. Neh 1–13), with close ties to Ezra (Ezra 1–10). Note on spelling: “Nehamiah” is commonly spelled Nehemiah in English Bibles.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Nicodemus appears only in John’s Gospel, in three scenes that trace a quiet but meaningful progression.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Noah’s story emerges from the widening corruption of humanity after Adam and Eve.
Obadiah — Bible Character Study (with attention to name ambiguity) Important note on identity: “Obadiah” (“servant of Yahweh”) is a fairly common Hebrew name.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Obed’s story is brief in narrative detail but massive in covenant significance.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Onesimus enters the biblical storyline in Paul’s letter to Philemon , written while Paul is imprisoned (Phm 1, 9–10, 13).

1) Story Summary (chronological) Onesiphorus appears late in the New Testament story, during the apostle Paul’s final known imprisonment in Rome (commonly associated with Paul’s “second” Roman imprisonment).
1) Story Summary (chronological) Paul first appears as Saul , a zealous Pharisee involved in the persecution of the early church.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Philemon is known to us almost entirely through the short New Testament letter addressed to him: Paul’s Epistle to Philemon .
Philip (the Apostle) — Bible Character Study Note: The New Testament mentions multiple men named Philip (e.
Important distinction: This study is about Philip “the evangelist” (one of the Seven), not Philip the apostle (one of the Twelve).

1) Story Summary (chronological) Philip the tetrarch appears only briefly in Scripture, not as an active character in a narrative, but as a historical marker anchoring the beginning of John the Baptist’s public ministry.
Name note: “Phineas” is often spelled Phinehas in many English Bibles. Scripture mentions at least two men named Phinehas: Phinehas son of Eleazar (grandson of Aaron) — the promine...
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Phoebe appears in Scripture in a brief but weighty introduction at the close of Paul’s letter to the Romans.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Pontius Pilate enters the biblical narrative as the Roman governor of Judea during the ministry of John the Baptist and Jesus (Luke 3:1).
Primary names: Priscilla (Acts) / Prisca (Paul’s letters) — same person (Acts 18:2; Rom 16:3).
Name: Quartus (Latin for “fourth”) Key text: Romans 16:23 Core limitation: Quartus appears only once in Scripture, so we must build the study primarily from that single verse and f...
Biblical data note: Quirinius is mentioned by name only once in Scripture (Luke 2:2).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Ruth’s story begins “in the days when the judges ruled” with a famine in Bethlehem that drives an Israelite family to Moab (Ruth 1:1–2).
First: which “Salome”? In the New Testament, the only woman explicitly named “Salome” is a follower of Jesus who appears in the passion and resurrection narratives (Mark 15:40; 16:1).

1) Story Summary (Chronological) Samson is introduced in the era of the judges, during a time when Israel repeatedly falls into sin and is oppressed by surrounding peoples—here, especially the Philistines (Judg 13:1).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Samuel’s story begins before his birth, with his mother Hannah’s anguish over barrenness and her vowed dedication of a son to the LORD (1 Sam 1:1–28).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Sarah (originally Sarai ) is introduced as the wife of Abram in the genealogy-to-narrative transition of Genesis.
Saul (son of Kish), first king of Israel — Bible Character Study (This study is about King Saul in 1 Samuel, not Saul of Tarsus/Paul in Acts.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Shem is one of Noah’s three sons, preserved through the flood judgment and positioned in Genesis as a key link in the covenant lineage that leads to Abram (Abraham).
Note on names: In Acts he is called Silas (Acts 15:22). In several epistles he is called Silvanus (1 Thess 1:1; 2 Thess 1:1; 1 Pet 5:12).
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Simon—later nicknamed Peter (Greek Petros , “rock”) and also called Cephas (Aramaic Kepha , “rock”)—begins in the Gospels as a Galilean fisherman a...
Identity note: Simon the Zealot is not Simon Peter. Simon the Zealot is one of “the twelve apostles” listed in the Gospels and Acts (Matthew 10:2–4; Mark 3:16–19; Luke 6:13–16; Acts 1:13).
1) Story Summary (chronological) Solomon enters the biblical story first as the son born to David and Bathsheba , named Solomon (and also called Jedidiah , “beloved of the LORD”) a...
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Sosthenes appears explicitly in only two New Testament places, both connected to Corinth .
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Stephen enters the biblical narrative in the early Jerusalem church, when a practical crisis threatens unity: Hellenist (Greek-speaking) widows are...
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Tabitha appears in a single, tightly focused narrative in Acts.
Thaddeus (also called “Judas, not Iscariot”) A note on names and identification (important) In the lists of the Twelve, Matthew and Mark include “Thaddaeus” (Matt 10:3; Mark 3:18),...
1) Story Summary (chronological) Thomas is one of the Twelve apostles chosen by Jesus (Matt 10:2–4; Mark 3:13–19; Luke 6:12–16).
1) Story Summary (chronological) Timothy enters the biblical narrative during Paul’s second missionary journey.
1) Story Summary (Chronological) Titus emerges in the New Testament as a trusted Gentile co-laborer of Paul—an emissary, problem-solver, and church organizer.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Tychicus enters the biblical record as one of Paul’s traveling companions near the end of Paul’s third missionary journey.
Name note (important): Most English Bibles call this king Ahasuerus in Esther (and in Ezra 4:6 ), while some translations (e.
1) Story Summary (chronological) Zacchaeus appears in a single, tightly focused narrative near the end of Jesus’ journey to Jerusalem.
Zechariah (father of John the Baptist) — Bible Character Study Important distinction: This study concerns Zechariah the priest , husband of Elizabeth and father of John the Baptist (Luke 1).
Important clarification (name overlap): “Zechariah” is a common biblical name. This study focuses on Zechariah the prophet , the contemporary of Haggai who prophesied during the re...
1) Story Summary (chronological) Zephaniah is a prophet in Judah during the reign of King Josiah (Zephaniah 1:1).

p.james-son-of-zebedee--26675adb.summary: Important identification note: The New Testament mentions multiple men named James .

p.jesus-of-nazareth--c4738d73.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Jesus Christ is introduced as the eternal Word/Son who was “with God” and “was God,” through whom all things were made (John 1:1–3; Col 1:15–17).

p.john-son-of-zebedee--b337fe0e.summary: Important identifiers: This study concerns John the apostle , brother of James (son of Zebedee) (Matt 4:21–22; Mark 1:19–20).

p.mark-john-mark-cousin-of-barnabas--091.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Where his story begins: John Mark first appears during the Jerusalem church’s early growth and persecution—at a moment when believers are gathered for prayer (Acts 12:12).

p.john-the-baptist--509f9f1e.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) John the Baptist enters Scripture as the promised forerunner who prepares Israel for the Lord’s coming (Luke 1:13–17; Isa 40:3; Mal 3:1).

p.joseph-husband-of-mary--1e97fe8c.summary: Note on identity: This is Joseph of Nazareth , husband of Mary, the legal father/guardian of Jesus (Matt 1–2; Luke 1–2)—not Joseph son of Jacob (Genesis).

p.joseph-of-arimathea--e7ff4ac4.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Joseph of Arimathea enters the biblical narrative late—at the crucifixion and burial of Jesus.

p.josiah-king-of-judah--fb8f42a7.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Josiah becomes king of Judah at eight years old after the assassination of his father Amon (2 Kgs 21:23–24; 22:1).

p.jude-brother-of-james--20c47f73.summary: Jude — Bible Character Study (with “Jude/Judas” name clarification) Important name note: “Jude” is the English form often used to distinguish Judas (Greek Ioudas ) from Judas Iscariot .

p.lazarus-of-bethany--12689b81.summary: Important note on names: The New Testament also includes a “Lazarus” in Jesus’ parable of the rich man and Lazarus (Luke 16:19–31).

p.luke-the-beloved-physician--05b89346.summary: Note on sources: Scripture gives only a handful of direct biographical references to Luke (Col 4:14; Phm 24; 2 Tim 4:11).

p.lydia-of-thyatira--8d5551e7.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Lydia appears in the book of Acts during Paul’s second missionary journey, when the Spirit directs the mission team into Macedonia (Acts 16:6–10).

p.john-mark--ac3d50bf.summary: Identity note: The NT “Mark” is almost certainly the same person as “John, whose other name was Mark” (often called John Mark ) (Acts 12:12, 12:25).

p.martha-of-bethany--ab33d3a4.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Where her story begins and ends in Scripture Begins: When Jesus enters a village and Martha welcomes Him into her house (Luke 10:38).

p.mary-mother-of-jesus--acfa811a.summary: (This study focuses on Mary of Nazareth , the mother of Jesus—distinguished from other Marys in the NT, e.

p.mary-magdalene--6dd9b3a6.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Where her story begins: in the Galilean phase of Jesus’ ministry, when she is identified as a woman from whom Jesus cast out seven demons and who b...

p.mary--e610363a.profile_name: Mary of Clopas (Mary the wife of Clopas)
p.mary--e610363a.source_path: NTCharacters/Mary of Clopas (Mary the wife of Clopas).md
p.mary--e610363a.summary: Name note: “Mary of Clopas” appears explicitly in John 19:25 . Many scholars connect her with “Mary the mother of James and Joses/Joseph” in the Synoptics ( Matthew 27:56; Mark 15:...

p.matthew-levi--e46c1088.summary: Note on name ambiguity: “Levi” can refer to (1) Levi the patriarch , Jacob’s third son by Leah (Genesis), and (2) Levi the tax collector , also called Matthew (Gospels).

p.matthias--ae401dfb.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Matthias appears in the Bible at a critical “in-between” moment: after Jesus’ ascension and before Pentecost.

p.nicodemus--6ff832aa.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Nicodemus appears only in John’s Gospel, in three scenes that trace a quiet but meaningful progression.

p.onesimus--e821d191.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Onesimus enters the biblical storyline in Paul’s letter to Philemon , written while Paul is imprisoned (Phm 1, 9–10, 13).

p.onesiphorus--10cdd616.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Onesiphorus appears late in the New Testament story, during the apostle Paul’s final known imprisonment in Rome (commonly associated with Paul’s “second” Roman imprisonment).

p.paul-saul--d4c08ff1.summary: Saul (son of Kish), first king of Israel — Bible Character Study (This study is about King Saul in 1 Samuel, not Saul of Tarsus/Paul in Acts.

p.philemon--59310849.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Philemon is known to us almost entirely through the short New Testament letter addressed to him: Paul’s Epistle to Philemon .

p.philip-the-evangelist--3ab8205e.summary: Important distinction: This study is about Philip “the evangelist” (one of the Seven), not Philip the apostle (one of the Twelve).

p.philip--6919aa55.summary: Philip (the Apostle) — Bible Character Study Note: The New Testament mentions multiple men named Philip (e.

p.phoebe--becf7925.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Phoebe appears in Scripture in a brief but weighty introduction at the close of Paul’s letter to the Romans.

p.prisca-priscilla--870a31f7.summary: Primary names: Priscilla (Acts) / Prisca (Paul’s letters) — same person (Acts 18:2; Rom 16:3).

p.quartus--b6adc1e0.summary: Name: Quartus (Latin for “fourth”) Key text: Romans 16:23 Core limitation: Quartus appears only once in Scripture, so we must build the study primarily from that single verse and f...

p.salome--d782b051.summary: First: which “Salome”? In the New Testament, the only woman explicitly named “Salome” is a follower of Jesus who appears in the passion and resurrection narratives (Mark 15:40; 16:1).

p.silas-silvanus--3ff90d56.summary: Note on names: In Acts he is called Silas (Acts 15:22). In several epistles he is called Silvanus (1 Thess 1:1; 2 Thess 1:1; 1 Pet 5:12).

p.simon-peter-cephas--d58cce37.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Simon—later nicknamed Peter (Greek Petros , “rock”) and also called Cephas (Aramaic Kepha , “rock”)—begins in the Gospels as a Galilean fisherman a...

p.simon-the-zealot--51ebe2d5.summary: Identity note: Simon the Zealot is not Simon Peter. Simon the Zealot is one of “the twelve apostles” listed in the Gospels and Acts (Matthew 10:2–4; Mark 3:16–19; Luke 6:13–16; Acts 1:13).

p.sosthenes-beaten--0683fab0.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Sosthenes appears explicitly in only two New Testament places, both connected to Corinth .

p.stephen--010f6a31.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Stephen enters the biblical narrative in the early Jerusalem church, when a practical crisis threatens unity: Hellenist (Greek-speaking) widows are...

p.tabitha-dorcas-raised--7aaf591e.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Tabitha appears in a single, tightly focused narrative in Acts.

p.thaddaeus-judas-son-of-james--26e88cd5.summary: Thaddeus (also called “Judas, not Iscariot”) A note on names and identification (important) In the lists of the Twelve, Matthew and Mark include “Thaddaeus” (Matt 10:3; Mark 3:18),...

p.thomas-didymus--dd3e1141.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Thomas is one of the Twelve apostles chosen by Jesus (Matt 10:2–4; Mark 3:13–19; Luke 6:12–16).

p.timothy--227fb1d9.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Timothy enters the biblical narrative during Paul’s second missionary journey.

p.titus--b5cd5081.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Titus emerges in the New Testament as a trusted Gentile co-laborer of Paul—an emissary, problem-solver, and church organizer.

p.tychicus--24c2a0b4.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Tychicus enters the biblical record as one of Paul’s traveling companions near the end of Paul’s third missionary journey.

p.zacchaeus--44b0f476.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Zacchaeus appears in a single, tightly focused narrative near the end of Jesus’ journey to Jerusalem.

p.zechariah--3b1fa613.summary: Important clarification (name overlap): “Zechariah” is a common biblical name. This study focuses on Zechariah the prophet , the contemporary of Haggai who prophesied during the re...

p.aaron--cc77e332.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Aaron enters the biblical story as Moses’ older brother from the tribe of Levi , appointed by God to be Moses’ spokesman in the confrontation with Pharaoh (Ex 4:14–16; 7:1–2).

p.abel.gene-4--fea33a69.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Abel is the second named human child in Scripture, born to Adam and Eve (Genesis 4:2).

p.abigail--5b7a820c.summary: Name note: Her name is usually spelled Abigail in English Bibles. Primary narrative: 1 Samuel 25 .

p.abraham--197f2a67.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Abraham (first called Abram ) enters the biblical story as a man living among the peoples of Mesopotamia, descended from Shem through Terah (Gen 11:10–27).

p.absolom--523258b1.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Absalom is a son of King David whose life traces a tragic arc: favored prince → avenger → estranged son → charismatic usurper → slain rebel.

p.adam.gene-2-5--6921e439.summary: 1) Story Summary Adam is the first man, directly created by God, placed in Eden to “work” and “keep” the garden, given a clear command regarding the tree of the knowledge of good a...

p.amos-of-tekoa--c656eb34.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Amos appears as a prophet unexpectedly raised up from ordinary work, sent with a severe message to the Northern Kingdom (Israel) during a season of...

p.boaz--a7b4c2d1.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Boaz enters the biblical narrative during the “days when the judges ruled” (Ruth 1:1), in Bethlehem of Judah (Ruth 1:1–2; 2:4).

p.cain.gene-4--931e91cf.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Cain is the firstborn son of Adam and Eve, born after humanity’s expulsion from Eden (Gen 4:1).

p.dan.gene-30-6--ed9d4f45.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Daniel’s story begins with his forced relocation from Judah to Babylon during the first wave of exile.

p.david--951bc327.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) David’s story begins as a young shepherd from Bethlehem, the youngest son of Jesse, chosen by God and anointed by Samuel to replace Saul as king (1 Sam 16:1–13).

p.deborah.prophetess-wife-of-lappidoth--.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Deborah appears during Israel’s cyclical period of the judges—after Joshua and before the monarchy—when “the people of Israel again did what was ev...

p.eleazar--b3d5a12a.summary: Eleazar — Which Eleazar? “Eleazar” is a common name in Scripture. This study focuses on Eleazar son of Aaron , who became high priest after Aaron (Num 20:22–29).

p.elijah--86b7f065.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Elijah enters Scripture abruptly as a prophetic messenger from the Lord during the reign of Ahab king of Israel , confronting national idolatry and...

p.elishah.gene-10-3-4--e3efa6b4.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Elisha enters the biblical narrative near the end of Elijah’s ministry.

p.enoch--50efb186.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Enoch (the descendant in Adam’s line through Seth) appears in the genealogy of Genesis 5.

p.eve.gene-2-5--7c858d94.summary: 1) Story Summary Eve is the first woman, created by God as a suitable helper for Adam, placed with him in Eden under God’s good rule and clear command (Genesis 2:18–25; 2:15–17).

p.buzi.father-of-ezekiel--646c146d.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Ezekiel is a priest turned prophet called by God in exile among the deported community in Babylon.

p.ezra-son-of-seraiah.scribe-priest--2e3.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Ezra enters the biblical narrative during the Persian period, after Judah has returned from Babylonian exile and the temple has been rebuilt (Ezra 1–6).

p.gideon--29c940b6.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Gideon enters the biblical narrative during a dark cycle in Judges: Israel again “did what was evil,” and the LORD gave them into Midian’s hand for seven years (Judg 6:1).

p.habakkuk--d7d46a7f.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Habakkuk is a prophet whose “story” is largely contained in the short book that bears his name.

p.haggai--2c135658.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Haggai appears suddenly in the post-exilic period as a covenant prosecutor and pastoral encourager.

p.hannah--06cd303e.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Hannah’s story unfolds in the opening chapters of 1 Samuel during the closing era of the judges, when Israel’s worship center was at Shiloh (Joshua 18:1; 1 Samuel 1:3).

p.hosea-son-of-beeri--8844380b.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Hosea is a prophet called by God to minister primarily to the northern kingdom of Israel (often called Ephraim ) during a period of outward prosperity but deep covenant unfaithfulness.

p.isaac.gene-17-25--66e8d49e.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Isaac is the long-promised “son of laughter,” born to Abraham and Sarah by God’s supernatural provision, and the covenant line is expressly carried through him (Gen 17:19; 21:1–3, 12).

p.aiah.gene-36-24--991b32de.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Isaiah is the major prophet most closely associated with Judah and Jerusalem in the generations leading up to the Babylonian exile.

p.jacob--4882490f.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Jacob’s story begins in the patriarchal narratives as the younger twin son of Isaac and Rebekah (Gen 25:19–26).

p.job--877e2717.summary: # Job — Bible Character Study ## 1) Story Summary (Chronological) **Where Job’s story begins and ends:** Job’s narrative begins with Job’s description and the heavenly council scen...

p.superscription-joel-son-of-pethuel--2f.summary: Note on identity: Several men named Joel appear in Scripture (e.g., Samuel’s son, civic leaders, genealogies).

p.jonah-son-of-amittai--edb9547f.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Jonah (“son of Amittai”) appears as a prophet of Israel during the reign of Jeroboam II (king of the northern kingdom), associated with a prophecy...

p.joseph.gene-30-24--e665eb01.summary: Scope note: This study is about Joseph the patriarch , son of Jacob/Israel (Genesis 37–50), not Joseph the husband of Mary (Matthew 1–2; Luke 1–2).

p.jeshua-son-of-jozadak.joshua--5d860647.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Joshua (Heb. Yehoshua , “Yahweh saves”) enters Scripture as Moses’ aide during Israel’s wilderness years, then becomes Moses’ commissioned successo...

p.malachi--da5b8f35.summary: Malachi — Bible Character Study Note on genre: Malachi is presented almost entirely through his prophetic messages rather than biographical narrative.

p.methuselah.gene-5-25-27--4783fcd7.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Methuselah belongs to the antediluvian (“before the flood”) patriarchs listed in Genesis’ genealogy from Adam to Noah.

p.micah-of-moresheth--4e9f3b2a.summary: Micah (the prophet, “the Morashtite”) Disambiguation: “Micah” can refer to multiple people in Scripture (e.

p.miriam--5f6df826.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Miriam is the sister of Moses and Aaron and a key female leader in Israel’s exodus generation.

p.moses--2c0fba85.summary: Moses — Robust Bible Character Study (Scripture-Primary) 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Moses’ story begins under Israel’s oppression in Egypt, when a pharaoh decrees death for Hebrew male infants (Ex 1:8–22).

p.nahum-of-elkosh--3b18a2d1.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Nahum is introduced as “Nahum of Elkosh” and identified with “the book of the vision of Nahum” (Nahum 1:1).

p.nehemiah-son-of-hacaliah.cupbearer-gov.summary: Primary texts: Nehemiah (esp. Neh 1–13), with close ties to Ezra (Ezra 1–10). Note on spelling: “Nehamiah” is commonly spelled Nehemiah in English Bibles.

p.noah.gene-5-32--6b1a74fb.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Noah’s story emerges from the widening corruption of humanity after Adam and Eve.

p.obadiah.prophet--4ac0ef81.summary: Obadiah — Bible Character Study (with attention to name ambiguity) Important note on identity: “Obadiah” (“servant of Yahweh”) is a fairly common Hebrew name.

p.obed--4192f2d2.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Obed’s story is brief in narrative detail but massive in covenant significance.

p.phineas--3c1a64cf.summary: Name note: “Phineas” is often spelled Phinehas in many English Bibles. Scripture mentions at least two men named Phinehas: Phinehas son of Eleazar (grandson of Aaron) — the promine...

p.ruth--8f3d9ec2.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Ruth’s story begins “in the days when the judges ruled” with a famine in Bethlehem that drives an Israelite family to Moab (Ruth 1:1–2).

p.samson--343c8a4f.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Samson is introduced in the era of the judges, during a time when Israel repeatedly falls into sin and is oppressed by surrounding peoples—here, especially the Philistines (Judg 13:1).

p.samuel--9a974328.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Samuel’s story begins before his birth, with his mother Hannah’s anguish over barrenness and her vowed dedication of a son to the LORD (1 Sam 1:1–28).

p.sarah--424e36f5.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Sarah (originally Sarai ) is introduced as the wife of Abram in the genealogy-to-narrative transition of Genesis.

p.shem.gene-9-26-27-10-21-31--1cfc9c3f.summary: 1) Story Summary (Chronological) Shem is one of Noah’s three sons, preserved through the flood judgment and positioned in Genesis as a key link in the covenant lineage that leads to Abram (Abraham).

p.solomon.son-of-david--c76a3c48.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Solomon enters the biblical story first as the son born to David and Bathsheba , named Solomon (and also called Jedidiah , “beloved of the LORD”) a...

p.zephaniah-son-of-cushi--ab737f2e.summary: 1) Story Summary (chronological) Zephaniah is a prophet in Judah during the reign of King Josiah (Zephaniah 1:1).
